clean_story:
Predicting next words. Mimicking human text.

The term large language model (LLM) describes AI trained on vast text to generate coherent responses—like a librarian who’s read millions of books but doesn’t understand them. Early versions, such as 2018’s GPT-1, strung together 117 million parameters (adjustable math "knobs"). Today’s models use trillions.

Here’s the mechanism: An LLM breaks input into tokens (e.g., "cat" = one token; "unicorn" = ["un", "icorn"]). It then calculates probabilities—"After ‘The sky is,’ what’s most likely: ‘blue,’ ‘falling,’ or ‘a spreadsheet’?"—using patterns from training data. No memory, no intent. Just statistics at scale.

Example: Ask an LLM, "How do I fix a leaky faucet?" It won’t know plumbing but will assemble plausible steps from repair manuals, forums, and videos it’s ingested. Errors slip through: A model might suggest "tighten the flux capacitor" if that phrase appeared near "faucet" in its training data.

Precision comes from size. GPT-4’s 1.76 trillion parameters let it handle nuance—detecting sarcasm in "Oh great, another meeting" 89% of the time (per 2023 benchmarks), vs. 65% for earlier models. Yet it fails at simple logic: "If all Bloops are Razzies and all Razzies are Lazzies, are all Bloops Lazzies?" Correct answer: yes. GPT-4’s accuracy? 78%.

Three related subjects are neural network layers, AI hallucination risks, and how transformers replaced RNNs.
